bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames Taylor’s win as a decisive, celebratory political victory—using terms like "cruised"/"romped," stressing a 5-2 liberal majority, policy consequences and even a reported 22-point margin—portraying momentum for Democrats; right-leaning pieces foreground ideological alarm—labels such as "Far-Left," "very liberal," "Democratic-backed" and urgency markers like "JUST IN"—and amplify an allegation she "allegedly violated electioneering laws"; center outlets de-emphasize rhetoric and focus on projection timing and margins; all agree Taylor won a 10-year seat, revealing deeper ideological and cultural fault lines.
summary: On Tuesday, Democratic-backed Chris Taylor defeated Republican-supported Maria Lazar, winning a Wisconsin Supreme Court seat and expanding the court's liberal majority to 5-2 through at least 2030.
The open seat arose following a conservative justice's retirement, building on the liberal majority Liberals first secured in 2023 after 15 years under conservative control.
Taylor centered her campaign on abortion rights, stating, "They want a court that prioritizes their rights and their lives." The race saw $6.5 million in total spending, far less than prior contests.
The expanded majority is poised to shape state policy on abortion, legislative maps, and election cases, with influence extending through the 2028 presidential election.
Liberals will have another chance to expand their majority next year, as conservative Justice Annette Ziegler has declined to run for re-election, potentially enabling a 6-1 court.
title: Source Icon

bias: Left coverage frames Fuller’s win as a defensive maintenance of the conservative quo—using 'held serve' and calling Greene 'controversial'—while right coverage casts it as a triumphant strategic gain, foregrounding 'Trump-backed' credentials and words like 'pads' and 'dashes Dem hopes' with 'showdown' urgency.
Rhetorically, the left favors restrained, contextual language; the right deploys celebratory, adversarial diction and sensational markers.
Center outlets add biographical detail and note the Trump endorsement more neutrally.
The dividing lines are the endorsement’s role and national stakes versus local predictability; all agree on the winner, the vacancy and Fuller’s prosecutor background.
summary: On Tuesday, Republican Clay Fuller won the special election runoff for Georgia's 14th Congressional District, securing 54 percent of the vote and filling the seat vacated by former Representative Marjorie Taylor Greene.
Greene resigned from her House seat in January following a public falling out with President Donald Trump over his handling of files related to the Jeffrey Epstein investigation.
Endorsed by the president, Fuller, a former district attorney and Air National Guard officer, campaigned on a 'MAGA America first' agenda against Democrat Shawn Harris.
Fuller defeated Harris decisively, securing 54 percent of the vote to Harris's 44 percent, with results called shortly after 8 p.m. on Tuesday.
The victory strengthens the Republican majority in the narrowly divided House, providing another ally as candidates ramp up campaigns ahead of the 2026 midterms.
title: Republican Clay Fuller wins special election for Marjorie Taylor Greene’s old seat in Congress

bias: Left-Leaning coverage emphasizes a narrative of presidential overreach and retreat—framing Trump's move as a reversal with charged phrases like "threats to raze Iran" and noting dramatic market effects —while right-leaning pieces frame the same outcome as a tactical win, using concessive language like "agrees" and "grants," spotlighting firmness in demands and security themes .

Pivotal divides include focus on presidential rhetoric versus operational details and third‑party mediation , with center coverage de-emphasized political heat and stressing technical coordination; all sides, however, report the two‑week conditional pause and the immediate oil-price reaction, reflecting deeper ideological and geopolitical priorities.
summary: Following President Donald Trump's announcement of a "double-sided ceasefire," Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi confirmed that ships may transit the Strait of Hormuz for 14 days under Iranian military coordination.

Before the conflict, the waterway operated under a decades-old system allowing over 100 ships daily through Iranian and Omani waters; recent drone strikes and projectiles forced tankers to avoid the passage entirely.
Crude oil prices crashed more than 15% to $95 per barrel after the announcement, falling from a $117 peak, while Dow futures spiked 1,000 points and the Nikkei is expected to open up nearly 3%.

Passage requires "coordination" with Iran's Armed Forces and consideration of "technical limitations," Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi stated, leaving investors questioning if Iran is truly loosening its chokehold on the Persian Gulf.

While the immediate threat of war may be pausing at the request of Pakistani intermediaries, the 14-day coordination period suggests the era of unimpeded navigation in the Strait has not yet fully returned.
title: Iran foreign minister says passage through the Strait of Hormuz allowed next 2 weeks under Iranian military management

bias: Left-Leaning outlets portray the ceasefire as a "humiliating retreat" from "apocalyptic" threats, highlighting how the base "freaks out" over the perceived "backflip.
Conversely, right-leaning media frames the pause as a "strategic victory" of "strength," asserting Trump "forced Iran to the table" through "strategic restraint.
While the left emphasizes the "annihilating" rhetoric, the right highlights the completion of military objectives.
Center-Leaning coverage focuses on the "diplomatic offramp" and market reactions, noting how indices like the S&P 500 rose.
Despite these ideological divides, all sides agree the two-week truce hinges on reopening the Strait of Hormuz.
summary: On Tuesday, President Donald Trump announced a two-week suspension of military strikes on Iran, contingent on Tehran agreeing to the "COMPLETE, IMMEDIATE, and SAFE OPENING of the Strait of Hormuz" following urgent mediation by Pakistani officials.

Pakistani Prime Minister Shehbaz Sharif and Field Marshal Asim Munir requested Trump hold back the "destructive force" scheduled for deployment that evening to allow diplomatic efforts to advance. Their intervention triggered the suspension just 90 minutes before the deadline.

Trump called Iran's 10-point peace proposal a "workable" basis for negotiation, triggering immediate market relief: Dow futures surged 1,000 points while U.S. crude oil plummeted more than 15% to settle near $95 per barrel from $117.

Iranian Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi confirmed Tehran accepted the pause and would allow safe passage through the Strait of Hormuz for two weeks. The nation's Supreme National Security Council, however, emphasized this does not signify the war's termination.

Negotiations are scheduled to begin in Islamabad as the two-week pause unfolds, though the truce remains fragile with both sides maintaining military readiness and offering conflicting accounts of the agreement's terms and scope.
title: Trump Agrees to Two-Week Iran Ceasefire to Finalize Talks

bias: Left outlets frame the incident as geopolitical signaling—frequent use of "Iran‑backed militia" and emphasis on a reported prisoner swap —while right outlets cast it as criminal terrorism , using terms like "terrorist/terrorists," "brazen daylight kidnapping," and urgent phrases such as "must leave immediately," spotlighting U.S. warnings and alleged "forced confession.
Left leans toward context and state‑level motives; right adopts law‑and‑order moralizing urgency.
Pivotal divides include swap details, prior warnings, and coercion claims; center outlets attribute claims and stress freelancer vulnerability.
Both sides agree she was released, the militia conditioned her departure, and officials confirmed it.
summary: On Tuesday, April 7, 2026, the Iran -backed militia Kataib Hezbollah released American journalist Shelly Kittleson in Baghdad, conditioning her freedom on her immediate departure from Iraq.
Iraqi and U.S. officials reported the release followed a prisoner exchange, trading Kittleson for several Kataib Hezbollah members previously detained by authorities for attacks on U.S. interests.

Surveillance footage captured the March 31 abduction, and the militia officially acknowledged responsibility for the first time, citing "in appreciation of the patriotic stances of the outgoing prime minister," Mohammed Shia al-Sudani.
Secretary of State Marco Rubio confirmed her release early Wednesday, stating the U.S. is "relieved" and working to support her safe departure from the country.
The Committee to Protect Journalists called the abduction an "alarming breach of journalists' safety," noting how Iran-aligned groups use kidnappings as leverage amid regional tensions.
title: US journalist Shelly Kittleson released by Iran-backed Kataib Hezbollah in Iraq

bias: Left coverage frames the pause as an 11th‑hour, ethically fraught de‑escalation—using charged labels like "genocidal threat," "seizes diplomatic offramp" and "humiliating demands" to stress legal and moral peril—while right coverage celebrates a strategic win with triumphant phrases such as "total and complete victory" and sensational language, emphasizing security gains and Israel's exclusion of Lebanon.
Left leans on moralizing expert critique and alarm; right uses triumphal, protector‑of‑security rhetoric and flags independent IRGC risks; center calls the pause "fragile.
Both agree on the two‑week ceasefire and Islamabad talks, underscoring deeper ideological and geopolitical divides.
summary: On Tuesday, President Donald Trump announced a two-week "double-sided ceasefire" with Iran, suspending military strikes after the Iranian government agreed to reopen the Strait of Hormuz.
The agreement came shortly before an 8 p.m. ET deadline Trump had set on Tuesday morning, when he threatened to destroy Iranian power plants and bridges if the government refused to open the strait.
Iran accepted the deal based on a 10-point plan, with talks scheduled for Friday in Islamabad, Pakistan, though Tehran emphasized "this does not signify the termination of the war."

Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu confirmed support for the ceasefire, though his office noted the agreement does not cover fighting against Hezbollah in Lebanon; protests erupted outside the White House.
Uncertainty remains regarding the deal's stability due to conflicting 10-point plans from Iran, while the conflict has displaced more than 1 million people and claimed more than 1,900 lives in Iran.
title: Israel has agreed to the terms of the two-week US-Iran ceasefire agreement, White House official says
With Minneapolis Medical Center’s Survival Threatened, Staff and Leaders Call for State Action

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the story around PETA ’s activism—using phrases like "long-running campaign"—and leans into the substantive allegation about "unhealthy 'standards'" for French bulldogs, while right-leaning pieces foreground the judicial outcome, employ the colloquial verb "tosses" to cast the suit as frivolous, and de-emphasize the judge’s stated legal reasoning .
Rhetorically, left uses advocacy language and a sympathetic tone; right adopts dismissive, skeptical phrasing; center reports the outcome bluntly.

The dividing line—whether to center PETA’s motives and health claims or the courtroom verdict—reflects deeper ideological splits over activism versus regulatory skepticism, though all agree the suit was dismissed and concerned breed health.
summary: On Monday, Judge David B. Cohen dismissed PETA 's lawsuit against the American Kennel Club, ruling that a New York law governing state and local government decisions was misapplied to the private organization.

PETA had accused the American Kennel Club of promulgating unhealthy "standards" for Frenchies, Shar-peis, bulldogs, dachshunds, and pugs, seeking to force the club to stop using breed guidelines linked to serious health ailments.

Club President Gina DiNardo hailed the decision Tuesday, while PETA founder Ingrid Newkirk criticized the ruling and urged people to adopt shelter dogs instead of buying purebreds bred "custom-made for a look that causes pain."
Cohen ruled the case "must be dismissed" because PETA is not subject to the kennel club's authority, leaving PETA lawyers assessing any legal options following the dismissal.
title: Judge tosses PETA’s lawsuit against the American Kennel Club over dog breed health

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the story as a legal development—highlighting he "pleaded guilty to trafficking cocaine" and an "agreement with the U.S. government" and using terms like "feared"; right-leaning pieces foreground criminal identity and law-and-order, amplifying "alias '85'," "massive trafficking" and an uncorroborated "captured and killed" claim to signal enforcement success.
The left uses restrained legal framing; the right uses villainizing labels and sensational tone; center de-emphasizes extra detail.
Key divides are El Mencho ’s reported fate and plea-deal specifics; all agree on the guilty plea and that CJNG is "powerful and violent," reflecting wider divides over crime and sovereignty.
summary: Erick Valencia Salazar, 49, of Santa Clara, California, pleaded guilty to conspiring to distribute cocaine for U.S. importation as a co-founder of the Jalisco New Generation Cartel.
Valencia Salazar faces a mandatory minimum sentence of 10 years and a maximum of life in prison for his role in the violent drug cartel.
The guilty plea marks a step forward in ongoing efforts by U.S. law enforcement to prosecute high-level figures tied to international drug trafficking networks.
title: Man Who Co-Founded Mexican Drug Cartel with 'El Mencho' Pleads Guilty in US to Conspiracy Charge

bias: Left-Leaning headlines emphasize technical specifics and repeated reassurance—naming flight #5001 and noting the "upper portion" opened and using "returned safely"—while right-leaning pieces favor concise, spokesperson-centered framing that highlights the plane "remained stable/continued operating normally" and de-emphasizes technical or passenger detail.
Centrist pieces inject human reaction, quoting "a bit nerve-wracking" or colloquial "popped open," increasing emotional tone.

Pivotal divides include whether to foreground flight number/door detail, passenger fear, or independent authority inquiry; all sides, however, echo the airline's account and the absence of injuries, revealing shared facts amid differing emphases.
summary: On Monday, Cape Air flight #5001 returned to Nantucket Memorial Airport after the upper portion of the main cabin door opened shortly after takeoff, prompting the crew to turn back immediately.
The aircraft , a Cessna 402C, is described by Cape Air as "the workhorse" of its fleet, typically used for regional routes like the roughly 30-mile journey from Nantucket to Massachusetts.
Passenger Fuller shared video on Instagram showing the open door mid-flight, later telling The Nantucket Current, "The pilot was amazing and made everyone feel calm."
Cape Air removed the aircraft from service for inspection, stating, "We are following all established safety procedures and will take any necessary actions based on our findings."
Passengers boarded an alternate aircraft and reached Boston safely; no injuries occurred during the incident, and the airline prioritized crew and passenger safety throughout the emergency.
title: Nantucket flight returns after part of a cabin door opens midair

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the ruling as preserving access—using phrases like "will remain available," "for now," and "setback"—emphasizing urgency, protection of telehealth/mail distribution, and portraying Louisiana’s suit as an attempt to "stifle" services.

Right-Leaning pieces stress regulatory failure and safety, highlighting the judge's rebuke with words like "scolded," warning of "mail‑order abortions," and deploying emotive condemnations such as "dangerous" and rhetoric about deaths; one noted a 6‑month FDA review deadline.
Center coverage is more procedural and de-emphasized partisan verdicts.
Both sides agree the judge paused enforcement and ordered an FDA review, underscoring divides over regulation, safety and reproductive rights.
summary: On Tuesday, U.S. District Judge David Joseph refused to block the mailing of mifepristone prescriptions, denying Louisiana's immediate request to halt the practice while the legal challenge proceeds.
Louisiana Attorney General Liz Murrill filed the lawsuit arguing that mail-order prescriptions undermine the state's abortion ban, one of 13 states that bar abortion at all stages of pregnancy.
One study found that by the end of 2024, one-fourth of abortions were accessed by telehealth—a fivefold increase in two years—making mifepristone central to post-Roe legal battles.
Murrill is pursuing criminal cases against two doctors in California and New York for mailing pills to Louisiana, though those states have not extradited the physicians to face charges.
Judge Joseph warned that his decision is not indefinite, ordering the Food and Drug Administration to update the court on its safety review within six months.
title: Judge Refuses to Block Sending Abortion Pill by Mail for Now, but Says FDA Must Finish Review

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the incident through a legal lens, emphasizing the "protest" context and intent to "start terror" while de-emphasizing suspects' identities.
Conversely, right-leaning outlets employ inflammatory rhetoric, using phrases like " ISIS -loving" to highlight a "jihadist" threat.
A pivotal dividing line is the desire to blow a "right-wing agitator" "in half," a detail de-emphasized elsewhere.
While the right uses the potential to kill "60 people" to amplify the sense of peril, center-leaning reports present it as a detail of the "ISIS-inspired" plot.
All perspectives converge on the indictment's core evidence, reflecting shared anxieties regarding radicalization and public safety.
summary: On Tuesday, federal prosecutors indicted Pennsylvania residents Emir Balat, 18, and Ibrahim Kayumi, 19, for an ISIS -inspired terror plot involving homemade bombs at Gracie Mansion on March 7.
Recordings revealed the pair planned to 'start terror,' with Balat telling Kayumi they calculated the devices could kill up to 60 people if the area was crowded.

U.S. Attorney Jay Clayton said investigators 'have uncovered evidence revealing the alleged meticulous planning by the defendants,' including a notebook with detailed attack plans and a storage unit containing explosive residue.
The incident occurred when an anti-Islam protest and counter-protest drew more than 100 participants; Balat threw an ignited device toward the crowd before both men were quickly detained.

Both men are held without bail after telling investigators they were inspired by ISIS; Balat hoped to carry out an attack bigger than the Boston Marathon bombing, which resulted in 'only three deaths.
title: Suspects Who Allegedly Threw Explosives Outside NYC Mayor Mamdani's Residence Indicted

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames Slotkin’s Iowa trip as outreach and pragmatic retail politics —using phrases like 'sits down,' 'picking the brains,' and 'Midwest pragmatism' while noting a health‑care town hall and 'would consider' 2028 speculation—whereas right-leaning pieces employ interrogative, accusatory tactics and negative labels , de-emphasizing 2028 talk and local policy; center pieces use tentative language and report meetings and organizing without partisan heat.
These dividing lines—meeting Trump voters, 2028 talk, and local policy—trace a broader outreach-versus-accountability partisan split.
summary: On Tuesday, U.S. Senator Elissa Slotkin held a paid focus-group lunch with five Iowa voters, questioning them about political divisiveness and community concerns as Democrats seek to regain Midwestern support.

Iowa swung from backing President Barack Obama in 2008 and 2012 to Republican President Donald Trump in the last three presidential elections, creating urgency for Democrats to understand voter shifts.
Klavins, a retiree from Urbandale, received $200 for attending the session, saying Slotkin's willingness to listen made her 'a little more genuine' in his eyes.
As a potential 2028 presidential contender, Slotkin urged Democrats to vet candidates early, while Iowa competes with Michigan for early nominating status in the altered primary calendar.
The Senator told reporters the 'old guard' of the Democratic Party is disconnected from voters, positioning herself as part of a new generation focused on Midwestern issues.
title: Sen. Elissa Slotkin sits down with Trump voters in Iowa while campaigning for Democrats

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the trip through sympathetic, damage-focused language—terms like "ease the pain" and "devastated" and criticism of prior leadership emphasize slow recovery and human impact, while right-leaning pieces highlight action and authority—phrases such as "reshapes DHS policies," "highlight storm relief efforts," and sensational, promotive turns present Mullin as decisive and policy-driven, especially on immigration and detention reviews; a pivotal dividing line is whether the story foregrounds blame/relief versus policy reversals, while the center’s $60 billion damage estimate and payback detail are de-emphasized by both sides; all agree he visited WNC, toured Helene damage, and pledged to speed FEMA relief, reflecting deeper ideological splits over government responsibility versus enforcement priorities.
summary: On Tuesday, Homeland Security Secretary Markwayne Mullin visited western North Carolina to address Hurricane Helene recovery, pledging to accelerate Federal Emergency Management Agency relief and clear project backlogs affecting roughly 2,000 pending approvals.

Last week, Mullin rescinded a rule requiring his personal approval for Department of Homeland Security expenditures over $100,000, which critics said had bottlenecked FEMA reimbursements and hampered disaster recovery efforts.

FEMA approved $26 million in home buyouts Monday to relocate families from flood zones, while the agency manages about $1.6 billion in obligated public assistance for North Carolina across roughly 2,000 projects still awaiting approval.

Mullin vowed to reform FEMA's bureaucracy rather than eliminate it, pushing back on administration calls to shut down the agency while promising shutdown-affected staff would receive missing paychecks by Friday.

Future DHS operations depend entirely on congressional appropriations, as the Disaster Relief Fund holds about $3.6 billion; Mullin also signaled potential immigration enforcement shifts, including possibly removing Customs and Border Protection officers from sanctuary cities.
title: Mullin pledges progress on disaster relief during his first official trip as DHS secretary
topic_full: U.S. Department of Homeland Security (DHS) · North Carolina

bias: Left-Leaning coverage emphasizes institutional alarms, citing an intelligence report that "warned" of a "persistent threat" to U.S. personnel, Jewish/Israeli institutions and Iranian dissidents and portrays the White House as having "downplayed" an "elevated threat.

Right-Leaning coverage similarly foregrounds warnings but balances them with reassurance—highlighting that the FBI/NCTC had not identified broad public dangers, framing a "disconnect" between internal alerts and public statements.
Center outlets attribute critiques to the president and use terms like "minimized.
Both sides agree an intelligence alert existed and note the White House's softer tone, reflecting priorities about accountability versus security framing.
summary: On March 20, the FBI issued a 'Public Safety Awareness Report' warning law enforcement that the Iranian government 'poses a persistent threat' to U.S. military personnel, government officials, and Jewish and Israeli institutions.

Iranian security services have attempted to kidnap and kill Americans in recent years, employing methods including 'stabbings, vehicle rammings, bombings, poisoning, strangling, suffocation, and arson,' the report stated.

Despite these warnings, President Donald Trump publicly minimized the possibility of such attacks, telling reporters on March 11, 'No, I'm not' worried, even as outlets reported the administration had previously blocked similar intelligence.

White House spokeswoman Abigail Jackson defended the administration's security efforts on Tuesday, urging media outlets not to 'irresponsibly sow fear by reporting on individual law enforcement memorandums.'

The report further stated that 'violent extremists with a variety of ideological backgrounds, including those who oppose the U.S. or Israel, also may see this conflict as a justification for violence.
title: Intelligence report warned of Iran's 'persistent threat' to US as White House downplayed the risk

bias: Left coverage frames the ten‑point paper as a U.S. concession, using triumphant, absolutist language like "lost the war" and highlighting the Farsi–English discrepancy over "acceptance of enrichment" to suggest obfuscation; right coverage instead portrays the plan as a list of coercive "demands," stressing strategic threats—"Hormuz Control," lifting sanctions —and warning of "deception" amid "escalating tensions," often centering a conservative negotiator as pragmatic.
Center outlets are more sourced and descriptive, de‑emphasizing triumphal or alarmist claims.
Both sides agree the plan, enrichment and sanctions are central and tied to a short ceasefire, revealing deeper ideological fault lines.
summary: The United States and Iran agreed to a 2-week truce on Wednesday after President Trump accepted a 10-point peace proposal as a "workable basis on which to negotiate."
Pakistan mediated the breakthrough after Trump warned a "whole civilization will die tonight" unless a deal was reached, averting massive American strikes on Iran.

Iran's blueprint demands "continued Iranian control over the Strait of Hormuz, acceptance of enrichment, lifting of all primary and secondary sanctions," plus United States military withdrawal from the Middle East .
Malaysian Prime Minister Anwar Ibrahim welcomed the plan but warned that "peace talks cannot succeed if the proceedings are cloaked in deception and double-dealing."
Anwar advocated for regional peace extending beyond Iran to include Iraq, Lebanon, and Yemen, emphasizing the need to end the dispossession of the people of Palestine.
title: Iran includes ‘acceptance of enrichment’ in Farsi version of its ceasefire plan, something missing from English versions

bias: Left outlets frame an accountability narrative—using phrases like "hundreds of complaints," "investigating," "neglect," and "devastating" to stress the 27 deaths and potential culpability—while right outlets adopt a procedural tone, repeating "investigating" and "license renewal" but de-emphasizing Texas Rangers, lawsuits, and political pressure.
Center coverage highlights Rangers' involvement, Lt.
Gov. calls and "legal battles.

Pivotal dividing lines are Rangers' participation, official calls to block relicensing, and lawsuit details; both sides, however, report regulators are probing and that last year’s floods killed 27 girls, reflecting deeper divides over oversight versus institutional normalcy.
summary: Texas Rangers join an investigation into allegations of neglect regarding the deadly Camp Mystic flood that killed 27 girls last July.
The Department of State Health Services received hundreds of complaints about Camp Mystic's operations and asked for help from state police .
Governor Dan Patrick urged the health agency not to renew Camp Mystic's license until investigations by Rangers and lawmakers are complete.
title: Texas Rangers Join Camp Mystic Flood Investigation Amid Neglect Allegations

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the case through a rehabilitative, empathetic lens—foregrounding "former Marine," "psychiatric treatment," and "wounded Marine veteran" to suggest service‑related trauma and mitigation—while right-leaning coverage stresses severity and accountability, highlighting "mass shooting," the scheduled "death penalty hearing," and portraying the judge’s order as superseding punitive options.
Center reporting leans legalistic, using procedural language like "incapable of standing trial" and citing court records.

The pivotal divides are veteran status and mental‑health explanations versus legal stakes and punishment; all sides nonetheless report the judge’s treatment order, three deaths, and first‑degree murder charges.
summary: On Tuesday, Superior Court Judge Jason Disbrow ordered Nigel Max Edge, a Marine veteran charged with three counts of first-degree murder, to undergo psychiatric treatment at Cherry Hospital in Goldsboro after determining he lacks capacity to stand trial.

Authorities allege Edge, a former Marine Corps scout sniper who served in Iraq, opened fire at the American Fish Company in Southport last September, killing three people and facing additional charges.

Brunswick County District Attorney Jon David said multiple mental health professionals concluded Edge currently lacks the "capacity to proceed", citing his traumatic brain injury and significant mental health issues.
The court's action suspends further litigation unless Edge's capacity is restored, David noted, though the state could still seek the death penalty if treatment succeeds.
Edge will remain in custody at Cherry Hospital for an "indeterminate" amount of time, with his trial timeline now dependent entirely on the success of his psychiatric treatment.
title: Former Marine charged in mass shooting is ordered to undergo psychiatric treatment

bias: Left-Leaning outlets frame the case as part of a punitive "mass deportation campaign," highlighting Abrego Garcia’s "wrongful removal" to El Salvador and naming the "Trump administration" to personalize blame, while right-leaning coverage foregrounds enforcement with terse verbs like "deport" and phrases such as "still intends/wants to deport," prioritizing stated government intent.
The left employs adversarial, emotive choices and includes advocacy voices; the right uses concise enforcement-focused diction and often de-emphasizes background like Liberia ’s rationale.
Centers stress legal procedure.
Both sides agree DOJ/DHS intends removal and cite the Costa Rica agreement, reflecting broader humanitarian vs. control priorities.
summary: The U.S. government still intends to deport Kilmar Abrego Garcia to Liberia despite a new agreement with Costa Rica to accept deportees who cannot legally be returned to their home countries.

Judge Paula Xinis previously barred U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement from deporting or detaining Abrego Garcia, referring to "one empty threat after another to remove him to countries in Africa with no real chance of success."
Abrego Garcia, who has an American wife and child and has lived in Maryland for years, argues that if he is to be deported, it should be to Costa Rica, which previously agreed to accept him.
title: US still wants to deport Kilmar Abrego Garcia to Liberia, despite new agreement with Costa Rica

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the 22‑year‑old’s nearly‑weeklong custody as personal harm and evidence of a "mass deportation campaign," stressing effects on "troop morale" and injustice; right‑leaning pieces foreground the subject’s military ties and emphasize enforcement, using terms like "freed" or simply "detained" to spotlight legal action; center coverage is more procedural, citing DHS that she "has no legal status" and providing context rather than moralizing.
Dividing flashpoints—arrest on a military base, military‑spouse status, and DHS’s legal rationale—trigger the sharpest partisan reframes, though all note detention, release, and public attention.
summary: On Tuesday, Annie Ramos, wife of a U.S. Army staff sergeant, was released from a federal immigration detention facility after spending nearly a week in custody at a Louisiana military base.
The Trump administration recently ended immigration enforcement leniency toward military family members, prompting backlash after agents detained Ramos on the Louisiana base.
Ramos has lived in the country since she was less than 2 years old and applied for Deferred Action for Childhood Arrivals in 2020, though her status remained stalled.
DHS released Ramos with a GPS monitor while she undergoes further removal proceedings. "She will receive full due process," DHS said.
title: Newlywed Wife of US Soldier Freed by ICE After Detention at Military Base

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames Blanche's remark as evidence of loyalty and DOJ politicization, highlighting words like "nobody," "fired," "showcased his loyalty," and the pledge to "root out weaponization," using an accusatory, skeptical tone; right-leaning coverage is de-emphasized in this sample, while center outlets attribute the quote and favor terms like "Only" and "replaced," framing it as a straightforward reply at an "unrelated news conference.
Pivotal dividing lines are portrayals of motive and weaponization and the lack of White House or Bondi comment; both agree on the core facts, revealing broader ideological trust gaps.
summary: On Tuesday, Acting Attorney General Todd Blanche held his first news conference, stating that "nobody" besides President Donald Trump knows why Attorney General Pam Bondi was replaced last week.

Bondi reportedly faced scrutiny over her failure to prosecute the president's political adversaries and stumbles in handling the investigation into Jeffrey Epstein, which remains under House Oversight Committee subpoena.

Blanche insisted Tuesday he feels no "pressure" despite the president's well-publicized desire for retribution, dismissing claims that the Justice Department is "weaponizing" itself as "completely false."
Addressing reports on two missing Air Force officers in Iran, Blanche noted the department "will always investigate" classified disclosures, even if it requires subpoenaing journalists.
Blanche, a former Trump defense attorney, demurred on his potential nomination as permanent Attorney General, stating that serving in any capacity under the president is "an honor.
title: Only Trump Knows Why He Replaced Bondi as Attorney General, New Leader of Justice Department Says

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the floating Nutella as a charming human-interest interruption—using phrases like "stole the spotlight," "rogue jar" or "best free ad ever"—that softens mission gravitas; right-leaning pieces cast it as a commercial win, using words such as "capitalizes," "greatest free advertising moment in history" and "wholesome moment" to tout brand benefit.

Pivotal divides are whether the jar signaled covert product placement or harmless publicity and how much to stress the milestone farthest-from-Earth; center coverage de-emphasized spectacle, noting it "makes a lot of sense" as astronaut food.
Both sides report viral clip and NASA's denial, revealing cultural splits over commercialization.
summary: On Monday, April 6, 2026, the Artemis II crew reached 252,752 miles from Earth, breaking the 1970 Apollo 13 record as a Nutella jar floated through the Orion spacecraft cabin.
Social media users joked that the hazelnut spread had traveled farther than any person, while others questioned whether Italian manufacturer Ferrero received unauthorized product placement.

In a statement to Futurism, agency press secretary Bethany Stevens stated NASA 'does not select crew meals or food in association with brand partnerships,' clarifying the sighting 'was not a product placement.'
Scientific American reports the crew brought 58 tortillas to pair with the spread, alongside beef brisket, broccoli au gratin, and scrambled eggs consumed during the mission.

Throughout the ten-day Artemis II mission, the crew faces technical challenges including persistent toilet issues and sleeping in cocoon-like bags, though the flight remains a significant moment in space travel.
title: NASA Denies Product Placement After Nutella Jar Floats Aboard Artemis II

bias: Left outlets frame Gates’ June 10, transcribed/closed‑door appearance as an accountability moment—using phrases like "will have to explain" and emphasizing institutional scrutiny—while right outlets foreground guilt‑by‑association and spectacle with labels such as "Epstein associate" or "pal" and verbs like "hauled" or "grilled," often amplifying personal admissions like a "huge mistake.
Center coverage de‑emphasizes drama, treating it as a procedural "interview" amid a "busy few months.
The pivotal split is culpability versus partisan targeting; all sides nonetheless agree on the scheduling, transcribed format, and that Gates "welcomes the opportunity.".
summary: On June 10, Microsoft co-founder Bill Gates will appear before the House Oversight Committee for a closed-door, transcribed interview regarding Jeffrey Epstein, with a representative saying he "welcomes the opportunity to appear before the Committee."

Republican Chairman James Comer requested the testimony on March 3, citing public reporting and Justice Department documents showing the committee "believes you have information that will assist in its investigation."

Justice Department documents contain unverified 2013 emails where Epstein claimed Gates engaged in affairs with Russian women and sought antibiotics; Gates' team called these allegations "absolutely absurd and completely false."
Gates admitted to foundation staff that spending time with Epstein was a "huge mistake" while maintaining he never witnessed or participated in any illegal conduct.

The Oversight Committee has broadened its probe into Epstein's network, recently questioning Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick on May 6 and former President Bill Clinton, with additional witnesses scheduled in coming weeks.
title: Bill Gates to appear before House panel in Epstein investigation

bias: Left-Leaning outlets portray the USPS as a "beleaguered" institution, framing the agreement as a "lifeline" that "secures" its future.
Conversely, right-leaning perspectives frame the shift as a logical business move, noting Amazon "reduces its reliance" on government services "as expected.

While the left highlights the 20% volume reduction as a victory over steeper threatened cuts—a detail de-emphasized by the right—the latter focuses on the "drop" in deliveries to emphasize efficiency.
Center-Leaning reports add regulatory context.
Both sides acknowledge the 1 billion package volume, reflecting ideological divides over public-sector sustainability versus private-market autonomy.
summary: Amazon .com has reached a new agreement with the U.S. Postal Service to retain around 80% of its existing deliveries with USPS, or more than 1 billion packages per year.
Amazon represented $6 billion in annual revenue to USPS, which has a roughly $80 billion budget.
The renewed delivery agreement, pending regulatory approval, is welcome news for USPS, as Amazon is its largest customer.
title: Amazon cuts back Postal Service deliveries in new agreement

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the episode as a moral imperative—highlighting calls to invoke the "25th Amendment," citing "evil" and "madness," and even noting "more than 70 lawmakers" backing removal—while right-leaning pieces portray Greene as politically errant, stressing that she "joins left-wing calls," election timing, and reproducing the dramatic "whole civilization" language to underline partisan spectacle; center outlets instead foreground security implications, warning the post could "escalate the conflict" and noting an "8 PM deadline.

Pivotal divides include the 25th's feasibility, the scope of congressional opposition, and operational timing—areas often de-emphasized—yet all agree Greene demanded removal after the threatening post, reflecting deeper ideological and geopolitical rifts.
summary: On Tuesday, Former Representative Marjorie Taylor Greene called for the 25th Amendment to be invoked, slamming President Donald Trump 's threat to "wipe out an entire civilization" as "evil and madness."

Trump posted on Truth Social that a "whole civilization will die tonight" amid the Iran war, declaring "complete and total regime change" would bring "revolutionarily wonderful" outcomes with an 8 p.m. deadline.

Despite mounting pressure, no Cabinet members have indicated willingness to invoke the amendment, while White House Press Secretary Karoline Leavitt stated the administration "will always act within the confines of the law.
title: Greene raises 25th Amendment after new Trump Iran post

bias: Left-Leaning coverage emphasizes institutional accountability and opacity—using terse verbs like "fired" and phrases like "refused to quit" to portray corrective action and highlight a closed‑door 20–30 minute meeting with few explanations—while right-leaning pieces frame the removal as partisan retaliation, deploying charged words like "ousts," "outrageous firing," "Evers‑Appointed," and invoking hearings and legislative backlash to suggest scandal.
Pivotal divides include claims about political motive , the brief private session and the unanimous 17–0 vote, which center outlets frame as "lost confidence.
Both sides agree on the abrupt removal, exposing deeper ideological and institutional fault lines.
summary: On Tuesday, the Universities of Wisconsin Board of Regents voted unanimously, 17-0, to fire System President Jay Rothman effective immediately, citing loss of confidence following an annual performance review.
Rothman countered he was blindsided, insisting his recent performance evaluation was "overwhelmingly positive" and that regents refused to cite specific reasons for the termination.

Rothman, a former chairman and CEO of the Milwaukee-based Foley & Lardner law firm, led the multicampus system since 2022 and brokered a 2023 deal with Republicans freezing diversity hires for construction funding.

Universities of Wisconsin Vice President for University Relations Chris Patton will serve as acting executive until an interim president is appointed, while the Senate scheduled a hearing for Thursday to review regent confirmations.
title: UW Board of Regents fires Universities of Wisconsin President Jay Rothman

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the board’s proposal as coercive and exclusionary—using charged words like "forced," "required," and "controversial" while emphasizing a lack of Black and Hispanic authors—whereas right-leaning pieces normalize inclusion, foreground "Bible stories" and use phrases like "restoring/protecting Western heritage" or "Biblically sound" to cast it as cultural reclamation; center reporting is more procedural, noting the 10–5 board majority and a possible 2030 timeline.

Pivotal divides center on church–state overreach versus cultural restoration and diversity concerns; both sides report the board is considering Bible passages that would affect about 5.4 million students.
summary: On Tuesday, the Texas State Board of Education—comprised of 10 Republicans and five Democrats—began considering proposals to add at least 15 Bible passages to the English curriculum for public school students.

Previously, Texas Attorney General Ken Paxton encouraged districts to adopt the optional Bluebonnet Learning plan, which offers $20 more per student in state funding than other state-approved materials.

The New York Times reports that proposed readings span every grade, including biblical stories like David and Goliath alongside established works such as "The Odyssey" and Martin Luther King's "I Have a Dream" speech.
Initial voting is expected this week, with a final decision scheduled for June; if approved, the new requirements for nearly 5.5 million students would take effect in 2030.
title: Texas Board of Education to consider requiring Bible passage readings for public schools

bias: Left-Leaning outlets emphasize systemic crisis, highlighting that "nearly 62%" of residents cannot meet living costs while portraying the "long-delayed" racial equity plan as vital.
Conversely, right-leaning coverage frames the initiative as "radical" and "fishy," using scare quotes around "racial equity" to signal skepticism.
While the left de-emphasizes the "DOJ pushback," the right portrays the removal of " DEI " language as an attempt to mask "lunatic policies.
Center-Leaning reports offer observational framing, though some note a "racial equity obsession.
Both sides acknowledge the staggering economic data, reflecting deep ideological divides over identity-based redistribution versus colorblind legality.
summary: On Monday, New York City Mayor Zohran Mamdani released the city's first Preliminary Citywide Racial Equity Plan, coordinating 45 city agencies to address systemic neglect and discrimination across municipal services.
Mandated by a 2022 voter referendum, the City Charter required the report to ensure accountability after the previous administration under Mayor Eric Adams failed to deliver it on time.

The True Cost of Living Measure found 62% of New York City residents cannot meet basic expenses, with families needing over $159,000 annually to achieve economic security despite earning just $124,000 median income.

Assistant Attorney General Harmeet Dhillon of the Justice Department's Civil Rights Division suggested the plan sounds "fishy" and possibly "illegal," signaling potential federal scrutiny of the equity initiative.

This rollout coincides with broader efforts by the Trump administration to restrict diversity, equity, and inclusion policies, including threats to withhold federal funding from states that do not comply.
title: NYC: Mayor Mamdani Unveils Racial Equity Plan

summary: On Tuesday, the Attorney General of Canada filed a court application seeking to overturn a Canadian Human Rights Tribunal decision that awarded compensation for alleged RCMP discrimination during a historical abuse investigation.

This legal challenge stems from a 2017 complaint by Lake Babine First Nation members, who alleged the RCMP engaged in discriminatory conduct while investigating historical abuse by a teacher in Burns Lake, B.C.

Directives issued by the tribunal last month require the RCMP to review policies regarding "Indigenous crime complainants in historical abuse investigations," though the federal government argues police investigations are not a "service" covered by the Canadian Human Rights Act.

The tribunal awarded $7,500 plus interest to complainants and witnesses, a decision the Attorney General claims is invalid because the Civilian Review and Complaints Commission is the "proper forum" for such discrimination complaints.

Arguing that criminal investigations function in the public interest, the Attorney General contends the tribunal "misinterpreted and misapplied" legislation, while complainants' counsel has not yet responded to the judicial review.
title: Federal government wants court to toss out human rights decision against RCMP
Skylines across the Middle East as ceasefire is announced
Houthis Confirm Coordination With Iran, Hezbollah In Several Attack Waves On Israel

bias: All outlets report that MEP Rima Hassan was summoned on suspicion of "apology of terrorism" and heard by police on two consecutive days, but frames diverge: left portrays the episode as political persecution, using charged terms like "judicial harassment," "abuse of law," "authoritarian slope," and blames a "pro‑Israeli lobby"; right emphasizes legality, stresses limits of "parliamentary immunity," disputes claims it was not "illegal," highlights alleged "drugs" found and accuses the extreme left of attacking the press; center amplifies the lawyer's "perfectly illegal" claim—disagreement thus centers on motive, legal interpretation, and media leaks.
summary: French prosecutors announced that Rima Hassan will face trial on July 7 over charges of 'promoting terrorism' related to a social media post. She also received summonses for a September 16 hearing on glorifying a crime and incitement.

Hassan's social media post last month referenced a 1972 attack in Tel Aviv that killed 26 people involving a Japanese activist. Prosecutors claim this reference constitutes 'promoting terrorism,' forming the basis for the charges.

Prosecutors allege CBD and the synthetic drug 3MMC were found in Hassan's possession during custody. On Friday, she denied the claims, stating one CBD product was legal while the second allegedly contained synthetic drugs added by unknown parties.
France Unbowed coordinator Manuel Bompard called Hassan 'the target of judicial persecution.' Hassan told her lawyer Vincent Brengarth the pattern of harassment 'began as soon as I entered politics.'

Hassan, the first French-Palestinian member of the European Parliament, has faced recurring controversy including an arrest during a Gaza aid flotilla and a fatal protest in Lyon. These incidents reflect heightened tensions in France over the Gaza conflict.
title: French-Palestinian MEP denounces 'judicial harassment'

bias: Left-Leaning headlines emphasize Starmer's diplomatic backing of a US‑ Iran "ceasefire", framing the trip as active "support and sustain" of peace, while right-leaning pieces stress stability and leadership, invoking "de‑escalation" language or injecting skepticism with phrases like "breaks silence" that imply evasiveness; center coverage shifts to operational concerns—"Strait of Hormuz" security—and uses calmer terms such as "welcome" or "moment of relief.
Fault lines include crediting the deal to "Donald Trump" and attention to maritime security versus humanitarian diplomacy.
Both sides nonetheless foreground the Gulf visit and endorse sustaining the ceasefire, reflecting deeper ideological splits over diplomacy versus security.
summary: Sir Keir Starmer travels to the Gulf on Wednesday to meet regional allies and discuss diplomatic efforts supporting the ceasefire between The United States and Iran.
Earlier on Tuesday, Donald Trump threatened to destroy Iran's bridges and power plants, warning that a whole civilization would die if Tehran did not meet his deadline.
Oil prices plummeted below $100 for the first time in days following the announcement, with both The United States president and Iran framing the ceasefire as a victory.
Speaking ahead of his visit, Starmer said the agreement will "bring a moment of relief to the region and the world" while working to re-open the Strait of Hormuz.
Together with partners, officials must support and sustain this ceasefire to turn it into a lasting agreement, consolidating diplomatic gains across the region.
title: Starmer to visit Middle East after US and Iran reach ceasefire deal

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the 47%/46% poll and surprising drop from a prior ~15-point win as alarming, using quotes like "gobsmacked" and words such as "plummeting" to stress shock; right-leaning pieces frame the same numbers as evidence of collapse, deploying superlatives and insults—"cratered," "worst," "far-leftist," "intellectual lightweight," "hateful Horde of Dems"—to dramatize failure.
Centers are more measured, noting "mixed reviews," historical norms and policy drivers .
Both sides agree approval has slipped and voters are divided, reflecting deeper ideological and cultural fault lines.
summary: A Washington Post-Schar School poll released Monday shows Virginia Governor Abigail Spanberger with a 47% approval rating, reflecting a near-even 47–46 split among 1,101 registered voters surveyed March 26–31.

Critics link the low approval to policy shifts including a redistricting referendum and immigration stances; Mark Rozell noted such polarization is unusual this early in a term after Spanberger's centrist campaign.

Spanberger's approval sits 13 percentage points below the average for Virginia governors in Post polling since the 1990s, trailing Republican Glenn Youngkin's 54% average approval at this point in his term.

Voters will decide the fate of a redistricting referendum on April 21 that could replace the current 6-5 congressional map with a 10-1 plan; Rep. Jen Kiggans, R-Va., characterizes it as a 'unilateral power grab.'

Nearly $40 million has been raised by the 'yes' campaign alongside $8 million by the 'no' side, with the referendum poised to dramatically shift Virginia's House delegation representation for the 2026 election cycle.
title: Spanberger drop in approval rating ‘stunning’: Sabato

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames Jones' remarks as urgent institutional alarm—highlighting language like 'free fall,' calls for the '25th Amendment,' 'dementia risk,' and labels such as 'conspiracy theorist' or 'unhinged' to delegitimize Trump—while right-leaning coverage centers Jones' emotional rupture, emphasizing he 'breaks down in tears,' needs an 'intervention,' and portraying him as a betrayed, embattled ally.
Center outlets de-emphasize sensational detail, using 'sick' or 'free fall' more sparingly.

The biggest dividing lines are calls for formal removal and warnings about Iran /nuclear risks, yet all sides agree Jones has turned on Trump, urged Republicans to 'cut bait,' and flagged fitness concerns.
summary: On Tuesday, far-right podcaster Alex Jones publicly urged supporters to abandon President Donald Trump, declaring the president is "gone" and requires an "intervention" due to health concerns.

Jones, a longtime MAGA supporter, specifically cited Trump's "massively swollen ankles" as evidence of heart failure, claiming the president's erratic decision-making regarding potential war in Iran reflects poor health.

Other former allies, including podcaster Joe Rogan and medical analyst Vin Gupta, have recently questioned Trump's mental acuity, marking a growing trend of disillusionment among conservative figures.
title: Alex Jones slams Trump’s ominous Iran threat: ‘That is the definition of genocide’
